coredata.dc:title: Construction of graphene quantum dots ratiometric fluorescent probe by intermolecular electron transfer effect for intelligent and real-time visual detection of ofloxacin and its L-isomer in daily drink
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Changxing
Qin, Feifei
Tang, Siyuan
Li, Xiameng
Li, Tingting
Guo, Guoqiang
Gu, Chenjie
Wang, Xu
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: The design of intelligent and real-time sensing devices is significant in the medical drug monitoring field, but it is still highly challenging. Here, ratiometric fluorescent detections of ofloxacin (OFL) and its L-isomer levofloxacin (LEV) constructed from tri-doped graphene quantum dots (T-GQDs) are reported, and the detection limits reach as low as 46/67 nM toward OFL/LEV due to the intermolecular electron transfer (intermolecular ET) effect. After adding OFL/LEV, the generation of electrostatic bond provides a channel for the intermolecular ET from the edge of T-GQDs to OFL/LEV, resulting in the fluorescence quenching at 414 nm and the fluorescence promoting at 498 nm. Furthermore, a smartphone can be used for the visual and quantitative detection of OFL and LEV by identifying the RGB values of test paper and drink samples. This work not only reveals the physics mechanism of ratiometric detection, but also develops a convenient smartphone diagnostic for OFL and LEV.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Intermolecular electron transfer
OFL/LEV detections
Paper-based sensors
RGB color analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and stability of T-GQDs
Ratiometric detections of OFL and LEV
The mechanism of T-GQDs based OFL/LEV detections
Analysis of OFL in practical samples
Visual assay of OFL and LEV using ratiometric fluorescent test paper

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of T-GQDs. (b) The application in OFL/LEV sensing, RGB color analysis and actual sample testing.

(a) TEM image of T-GQDs. Inset: high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image. (b) The corresponding particle size distribution. (c) AFM image of T-GQDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of T-GQDs and precursor norfloxacin. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (e), O 1 s (f), N 1 s (g), P 2p (h) and F 1 s (i).

(a) UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of T-GQDs. The insets show the optical images of T-GQDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and a 365 nm illumination (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of T-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for T-GQDs. Fluorescence intensity, excitation and emission wavelengths of T-GQDs in different solvents (d) and along with different concentrations (e). The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of T-GQDs under (f) pH = 1–14, (g) ultraviolet irradiation for different timescales (150 W and 365 nm mercury lamp), different (h) ionic strengths of NaCl aqueous solution and (i) storage time.

(a-d) Excitation-emission matrix graph maps for T-GQDs with the increase of OFL concentration. (e) The selectivity of the T-GQDs to various common metal ions, amino acids and molecules. (f) Fluorescence spectra of T-GQDs with the addition of different kinds of FQs. (g) Fluorescence emission spectra of T-GQDs with different concentrations of OFL. (h-i) The corresponding relationship between F 2/F 1 and the OFL concentration. (j) Fluorescence emission spectra of T-GQDs with different concentrations of LEV. (k-l) The corresponding relationship between F 2/F 1 and the LEV concentration.

(a) Relationship between the OFL concentration and fluorescence intensity changes of OFL with and without T-GQDs. (b) Relationship between OFL concentration and fluorescence emission wavelength of OFL with and without T-GQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of T-GQDs in the presence and absence of OFL/LEV. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of T-GQDs with different concentrations of OFL. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of OFL with different concentrations. (f) Tauc-Plot for bandgap calculation in T-GQDs with different OFL concentrations. (g) Overall luminescence schematic diagrams of the T-GQDs sensing OFL/LEV. The time-resolved PL spectra of the T-GQDs in the absence and presence of OFL monitored at 414 nm (h) and 498 nm (i) under the 334 nm excitation.

(a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process of obtaining G and B values by a smartphone application (ColorDesk). The fluorescence images of paper-based sensor for the selectivity (b), sensitivity (c) and real sample detection (d) of OFL/LEV. All the images are taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. (e) The corresponding relationship between G/B value and the OFL concentration. (f) Schematic diagram of the evaluation for the practical application capability.

head.ce:title: Construction of graphene quantum dots ratiometric fluorescent probe by intermolecular electron transfer effect for intelligent and real-time visual detection of ofloxacin and its L-isomer in daily drink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Telecommunications and Information Engineering, Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Nanjing 210003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Telecommunications and Information Engineering
Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Telecommunications and Information Engineering, Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Nanjing 210003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of intelligent and real-time sensing devices is significant in the medical drug monitoring field, but it is still highly challenging. Here, ratiometric fluorescent detections of ofloxacin (OFL) and its L-isomer levofloxacin (LEV) constructed from tri-doped graphene quantum dots (T-GQDs) are reported, and the detection limits reach as low as 46/67 nM toward OFL/LEV due to the intermolecular electron transfer (intermolecular ET) effect. After adding OFL/LEV, the generation of electrostatic bond provides a channel for the intermolecular ET from the edge of T-GQDs to OFL/LEV, resulting in the fluorescence quenching at 414 nm and the fluorescence promoting at 498 nm. Furthermore, a smartphone can be used for the visual and quantitative detection of OFL and LEV by identifying the RGB values of test paper and drink samples. This work not only reveals the physics mechanism of ratiometric detection, but also develops a convenient smartphone diagnostic for OFL and LEV.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 1970s, the third-generation fluoroquinolones (FQs) have been the most popular antibacterial drugs for over 50 years (Li et al., 2018). To date, pharmacologists have synthesized many kinds of FQs used in a variety of veterinary and human medicines for the treatment of various pathogenic bacterial infections, such as lower respiratory, pulmonary and urinary tract infections (PharmD, Stephanie, Julie, & PharmD, 2022). Among these FQs, ofloxacin (OFL) can provide effective treatment against various infections caused by gram-negative organisms (Fang et al., 2022). However, the widespread use of OFL will inevitably cause adverse effects, such as tendon damage and peripheral neuropathy. In addition, due to the poor biodegradation property of OFL, it can contaminate the environment and cause the long-term absorption by livestock animals, which eventually makes OFL residues appear in human bodies through the food chain (Shi, Zou, Wang, Wang, Fan, & Gong, 2022; Maia, Ribeiro, Amorim, Barreiro, Cass, Castro, & Tiritan, 2014). Levofloxacin (LEV), the optical L-isomer of OFL, has been demonstrated to be biotoxic when overdosed, which can lead to tendonitis, tendon rupture and long-term sequelae (Schwarz, Grehn, Temming, Holz, & Eschenhagen, 2021; Davis, & Bryson, 1994). Considering the potential hazards caused by the residues of these two types of FQs in the living environment and daily drinking products, the European Union (EU) has restricted the maximum level of FQs and the maximum residue limit in food (below 100 µg/kg) (Heidarian et al., 2021; Suanchan, Chansud, Sa-nguanprang, & Bunkoed, 2021). Consequently, it is essential to propose a convenient and efficient method for the fast detection of OFL/LEV residues in the environment.

Generally, an excellent chemical analytical method should contain three basic factors: cheapness, convenient analytical equipment and high selectivity/sensitivity. Until now, a variety of analytical methods, such as chemiluminescence, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), cyclic voltammetry capillary electrophoresis and spectrophotometry, have been utilized for the detection of OFL/LEV (Vakh, Likanov, & Bulatov, 2021; Hu et al., 2021; Wang, Sun, Cai, Wang, Zhang, Fang, & Wang, 2022; Nguyen, Le, Dong, & Pham, 2018). However, one or more drawbacks exist in these methods, such as the huge consumption of raw materials, complicated equipment and high cost of acquisition. Moreover, the identification of OFL/LEV from FQs is difficult since most analytical methods ignore the selectivity of OFL/LEV detections from FQs due to their similar chemical structures (Zhang, Xie, Yu, & Cheng, 2019). Hence, it is really important to develop effective materials or methods with a rapid process, good sensitivity, outstanding selectivity and high-throughput to trace the level of LEV/OFL in the actual food samples.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as an emerging type of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted a wide range of interest because of their abundant sources, tunable optical properties, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2021). For sensing applications, in particular, a series of GQDs based nano sensors have been designed for the detection of temperature, pH, DNA, antibiotics, cations, cancer cells and antibiotics (Wang et al., 2021; Bagheri, Yousefi, & Mehrmashhadi, 2019). However, the ordinary single-emission peak sensing based on GQDs usually exhibits poor selectivity and low sensitivity, while high quality ratiometric fluorescent probes are rarely reported. More importantly, the sensing analytes have different effect on the emission peak intensities of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which is usually accompanied by a change of the fluorescent color to enhance the visualization contrast (Lesani, Singh, Lu, Mirkhalaf, New, & Zreiqat, 2022). Thus, GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors with dual emissions are highly expected because of their superior capability of robust and noninvasive detection even in a complex or disturbing environment (Jalili, Khataee, Rashidi, & Razmjou, 2020). Although GQDs-based sensors for the detection of FQs have been reported in many works, most of them can only provide a single fluorescence signal, which is susceptible to interference from environmental impurities, making the results unreliable in practical applications (Li, et al., 2018). Additionally, GQDs are easily incorporated into solid substrates (such as paper, hydrogels and polymers), constructing solid-state sensing devices with convenient and real-time monitoring capabilities (Wu et al., 2021). Therefore, in order to break the bottleneck of traditional single-light analysis method, it is crucial to develop high-performance GQDs with ratiometric solid-state sensing for real-time detection of OFL/LEV, which has strongly practical significance in reducing environmental pollution and monitoring human health.

As discussed above, we propose a novel and intelligent fluorescent colorimetry strategy for the sensitive detection of OFL/LEV via tri-doped GQDs (T-GQDs) with dopants of fluorine, nitrogen and phosphorus atoms. T-GQDs are synthesized by a one-step method using norfloxacin and phosphoric acid as doping sources (Fig. 1
(a)). Systematical characterizations reveal that the as-prepared T-GQDs show ultra-stable photoluminescence ability and concentration-adjusted colorful emission. Unexpectedly, when OFL/LEV is added to T-GQDs solution, the PL spectrum of T-GQDs changes from single peak to double peak emissions (414 nm and 498 nm) under the excitation of 334 nm wavelength laser. Besides, as the OFL/LEV concentration increases, the peak at 414 nm gradually decays while the peak at 498 nm increases inversely. From the systematic experimental data, we speculate that the electrostatic bonds formed between T-GQDs and OFL/LEV lead to the generation of the intermolecular electron transfer (intermolecular ET) effect, which enables electron transfer from T-GQDs to OFL/LEV. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on T-GQDs has been constructed to detect OFL and LEV with the ultralow detection limits of 46 nM and 67 nM, respectively. In order to solve the complex process of inspection, an innovative and intelligent RGB-color analysis method based on fluorescent test paper has been developed and can be conveniently used for the real-time detection of OFL/LEV (Fig. 1 (b)). To the best of our knowledge, this RGB color analysis based on test paper for the detection of OFL/LEV has never been reported before. Finally, we successfully apply the ratiometric probe in the identification of OFL in daily drinks including juice, carbonated drink and milk. In summary, these results show that the proposed T-GQDs-based sensing platform provides great potential to detect FQs in daily food and environment.

The chemical reagents, such as norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, ofloxacin, levofloxacin, fleroxacin, marbofloxacin, pefloxacin, formamide, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol, acetone, acetonitrile, ethyl acetate, K2CO3, AgNO3, NaCl, C4H6CoO4, NH4Cl, MgCl2, CuCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, FeCl3, Al(NO3)3, L-Alanine, L-Serine, L-Proline, L-Aspartic, L-Cysteine, glucose and dopamine were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethanol was purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Deionized water throughout all experiments was obtained from the Millipore system.

Synthesis of T-GQDs was prepared by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, the mixture of 1 mmol norfloxacin and 1 mL phosphoric acid was dissolved in 30 mL water, and then the solution was subjected to ultrasound treatment for 20 min. After dispersion, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave under the heat treatment of 200 °C for 8 h. After the reacted solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature, it was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Dilute the obtained solution with deionized water to keep the pH of the solution in the range of the 6–9, then dialyzed deionized water using a 500 Da cellulose membrane to remove unreacted molecules. Finally, the T-GQDs were obtained after freeze drying.

Microstructures and size distributions of T-GQDs were characterized using a JEOL-2010 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan). Fluorescent spectra and UV–vis spectroscopy were got in Hitachi FL7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer instrument and Shimadzu UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime data were collected by the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a XD-2X/M4600 was used for the microwave digestion. Raman spectrum (HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR 800) of T-GQDs was obtained using an Ar+ laser with the wavelength of 532 nm. Chemical bonds and components of T-GQDs were investigated by a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer in the frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, PHI Quantera II system, Ulvac-PHI, Ltd., Japan).

Detections of OFL and LEV using T-GQDs were performed at room temperature in deionized water. First, the aqueous solution of T-GQDs with a concentration of 0.24 mg/mL was prepared. Subsequently, 1.5 mL OFL and LEV with different concentrations were added into the prepared 1.5 mL T-GQDs solution (0.24 mg/mL), which was then incubated for 20 s. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature under an excitation wavelength of 334 nm. To improve the selectivity of OFL/LEV detection, norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, fleroxacin, marbofloxacin, pefloxacin, K+, Ag+, Na+, Co2+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, L-Alanine (L-Ala), L-Serine (L-Ser), L-Proline (L-Pro), L-Aspartic (L-Ala), L-Cysteine (L-Cys), glucose and dopamine (DA) were selected to evaluate the selectivity of T-GQDs. All the sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted 15 times.

Milk samples were purchased from the local market and pre-treated as follow (Li, Liu, Zhang, Luo, Lin, & Jiang, 2021). In brief, milk (2 mL) and acetonitrile (10 mL) were mixed and sonicated for 15 min. Then, the mixture was centrifuged to remove the pellet of denatured protein. The supernatants were collected and filtered through a 0.22 μM organic filter membrane to remove protein residues. Besides, the treated milk samples were diluted 10-fold by water. In contrast, there was no need to do further processing for juices and carbonated drinks.

One typical kind of T-GQDs with dopants of fluorine, nitrogen and phosphorus atoms is prepared via a simple hydrothermal method. Fig. 2
(a) shows the representative TEM images of as-prepared sample. It can be seen that all the materials are dot-shaped and finely dispersed without obvious aggregation. The high-resolution TEM image (the inset image of Fig. 2 (a)) illustrates the high crystallinity structure of T-GQDs with near well-resolved lattice fringes. The crystal plane spacing of 0.215 nm corresponds to the (101) graphite plane (Gao, Huang, Tan, Lv, & Zhou, 2022). The size of T-GQDs ranges from 4.5 to 8.5 nm and the average lateral diameter is 6.40 ± 0.06 nm (Fig. 2 (b)). In addition, the AFM image also indicates that the T-GQDs are monodispersed with a vertical height of about 1.5 nm (Fig. 2 (c)). Nearly spherical shapes of the T-GQDs have been demonstrated from TEM and AFM measurements. Two peaks of T-GQDs in the XRD pattern are clearly observed, a strong peak at 2θ = 21° and a weak peak at 2θ = 44.5° ( Fig. S1
(a)). The weak peak is assigned to the (101) facet of graphitic carbon, which corresponds to lattice fringes with 0.215 nm in HR-TEM images (Ansi, & Renuka, 2018). Raman spectrum of T-GQDs shows the characteristic D band at 1355 cm−1 and G band at 1574 cm−1 ( Fig. S1
(b)). Remarkably, the intensity ratio I G/I D is about 1.2, thus further confirming the high crystallinity of the T-GQDs (Xu et al., 2022).

FT-IR measurement is then applied to recognize the functional groups on the surface of T-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (d), two broad absorptions at 3432 cm−1 and 2909 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH and CH, respectively, which are derived from the precursor norfloxacin. The absorption peaks at 2652 cm−1, 1639 cm−1 and 1535 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of P-OH, CO and CC, respectively (Su, Lu, & Yang, 2019). The CN vibrational absorption band and C-F absorption peak of T-GQDs are observed at 1339 cm−1 and 1033 cm−1, respectively, which reveal that T-GQDs inherit nitrogen and fluorine related functional groups from norfloxacin (Liu, & Wang, 2009). Meanwhile, T-GQDs display a sharp FT-IR absorption band at about 685 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of PC. These FT-IR consequences can be further verified by the XPS analysis. As depicted in Fig. S2 and Table S1 , there are five typical peaks in the XPS spectrum of T-GQDs: C 1 s (284.6 eV), N 1 s (400.3 eV), O 1 s (532.9 eV), P 2p (134.1 eV) and F 1 s (687.6 eV), and the atomic ratios are calculated to be 71.6 %, 6.3 %, 11.5 %, 6.7 % and 3.9 %, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum of T-GQDs in Fig. 2 (e) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at nearly 284.4 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.7 eV, which are the characteristic CC/CC, CO/CO and C-F/CN, respectively (Wang et al., 2021). Furthermore, the deconvolution of O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2 (f) produces two distinct peaks at 513.5 eV and 532.9 eV, which are assigned to CO/PO and CO, respectively. Besides, the N 1 s band (Fig. 2 (g)) exhibiting three peaks at 399.5 eV, 400.8 eV and 401.8 eV confirms the presence of pyridinic N, amino N and graphitic N groups, respectively. In Fig. 2 (h), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the P 2p band is separated into two peaks at 134.2 eV and 134.8 eV, which are assigned to PO and PC, respectively (Wang, Peng, Li, Su, Chen, & Chen, 2018). The F 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2 (i) shows only one peak at a binding energy of 687.6 eV due to the covalent C-F in T-GQDs (Liu et al., 2019). These results are consistent with our FT-IR data. Combined with the results of XPS and FT-IR spectra, it is confirmed that hydrophilic functional groups exist on the surface of the as-prepared T-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of T-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the optical properties of T-GQDs, the UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission measurements are performed as shown in Fig. 3
(a). In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of T-GQDs, two basic absorption peaks at 228 nm and 268 nm are ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 carbon core. Two broad absorption peaks at 318 nm and 336 nm can be appointed to n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO groups (Wang et al., 2021). The weak absorption shoulder at 355–400 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of C-F/CN on the surface of T-GQDs (Long et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the best excitation wavelength at 385 nm in the excitation spectrum of T-GQDs is accordance with the absorption band at 355–400 nm, indicating that the optimal emission peak (443 nm) should be resulted from the surface groups of T-GQDs (CN/C-F). Furthermore, a wavelength-dependent behavior of the T-GQDs is observed at the excited wavelength of 300–400 nm (Fig. 3 (b)), further implying that the fluorescence primarily originates from functional surface chemical groups (C-F/CN). Fig. 3 (c) shows the corresponding excitation-emission matrix of the T-GQDs solution and demonstrates a single fluorescence emission center (385 nm, 443 nm).

In order to gain further insight into the PL stability of T-GQDs, the systematic evaluation is carried out under different conditions, including the solvent environment, concentration, pH, ionic strength, UV irradiation and storage time. As illustrated in Fig. S3 , the optimal PL intensities, excitation and emission wavelengths of T-GQDs solutions are measured. In Fig. 3 (d), except for alcoholic solvents (ethanol and methanol), the emission wavelength has a slight blue shift with the decreasing of solvent polarity, which is consistent with previously reported literature (Wang, Wu, Xing, & Wang, 2022). Notably, compared with other solvents, T-GQDs in aqueous solution exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity, suggesting that T-GQDs are highly feasible in practical applications with the background environment of water. In addition to the solvent stability, the concentration effect is investigated as shown in Fig. 3 (e). The emission wavelength of the synthesized T-GQDs shifts from 410 to 478 nm when the concentration varies from 0.05 to 2.50 mg/mL. The same trend also occurs in the corresponding excitation wavelength, which shifts from 330 to 411 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity increases and then decreases once the concentration reaches 0.65 mg/mL. The fluorescence results of T-GQDs at different concentrations are presented in Fig. S4 and Table S2 . Based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE), the shorter spacing of T-GQDs will contribute to the redshift of emission wavelength and the change of PL intensity (Zhang, Zhuo, Yin, Fan, Zhang, Liu, & Chen, 2019). In addition, as the pH changes from 1 to 14, the emission wavelength of prepared T-GQDs is relatively stable. However, the PL intensity is decreased significantly (Fig. 3 (f) and Fig. S5 ), which can be explained by the fact that the deprotonation degree of PO and –NH2 functional groups increases with the increase of alkalinity. The broken hydrogen bonds and reduced structural rigidity result in a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Su, Lu, & Yang, 2019). Summarily, the robust photo-, ions- and time-stability in this work (Fig. 3 (g-i)) indicate that the as-prepared T-GQDs are particularly appropriate for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detections of OFL and LEV
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical applicability of T-GQDs has been examined to test their possible application as sensors. Astonishingly, as a label-free fluorescent probe, T-GQDs can detect OFL and its L-isomer LEV directly. As seen in Fig. 4
(a-d) and Fig. S6 , when the concentrations of OFL and LEV in T-GQDs solution gradually increase, two-dimensional fluorescence spectrum scanning of the mixture presents an unusual transition from a single fluorescence center to dual fluorescence centers. Meanwhile, the intensities of the two fluorescence centers are competing with each other, in which the emission peak at 414 nm fades while the fluorescence intensity of 498 nm increases obviously. The fluorescence peak at 498 nm is assigned to the fluorescence peak of OFL/LEV, while the fluorescence peak at 414 nm is assigned to T-GQDs (Li, et al., 2018). Therefore, this phenomenon demonstrates that the T-GQDs can be employed as a ratiometric sensor for sensing OFL and LEV.

To understand the selectivity of T-GQDs for sensing OFL and LEV, the fluorescence intensity at 414 nm for T-GQDs is investigated in the presence of different kinds of interferences with the concentration of 1 M under identical conditions, including 14 common metal ions (K+, Ag+, Na+, Co2+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Al3+) and 7 common molecules (L-Ala, L-Ser, L-Pro, L-Ala, L-Cys, glucose, DA). It is obvious that OFL or LEV can induce strong fluorescence quenching for T-GQDs at 414 nm, while those interfering substances show almost no quenching as demonstrated in Fig. 4 (e). In addition, the selectivity of T-GQDs is also evaluated under different classes of FQs (norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, fleroxacin, marbofloxacin and pefloxacin) as depicted in Fig. 4 (f). The addition of a majority of FQs in T-GQDs solution produces a direct quenching of fluorescence, which is significantly different from the appearance of double fluorescence region for the addition of OFL and LEV. These results demonstrate that the T-GQDs have good specificity for the determination of OFL and LEV from FQs, which is rare in other related literature (Heidarian et al., 2021).

Generally, experimental conditions including the concentration of T-GQDs, response time and pH are optimized to achieve high sensitivity before the detections of OFL/LEV. Considering the concentration-induced red-shift behavior of T-GQDs, the optimization of solution concentration is essential and performed. As shown in Fig. S7 , with 300 μM OFL and 600 μM LEV in T-GQDs, the ratio of F 2/F 1 (“F 1” and “F 2” are defined as fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs at 414 nm and 498 nm, respectively) trends to increase and then decreases with the increase of T-GQDs concentration, reaching the maximum value at the T-GQDs concentration of 0.24 mg/mL. In Fig. S8 , it is shown more intuitively that T-GQDs solution with a low concentration is more favorable for colorimetric detection because of the larger peak spacing. In addition, the OFL/LEV detection can be finished within 20 s ( Fig. S9 ), which is highly important in the sensing. As illustrated in Fig. S10 , with the increase of OFL/LEV concentrations, the ratio of F 2/F 1 has the best linear fitting degree at pH = 7. In view of the above three situations, the optimal conditions of T-GQDs for the detection of OFL/LEV are 0.24 mg/mL, 20 s and pH = 7, respectively.

Under the best experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs at 414 nm and 498 nm are measured to evaluate the sensitivity of the T-GQDs-based sensor. As presented in Fig. 4 (g), the variation of the two peak intensities of T-GQDs solution stimulated by different concentrations of OFL is obvious. The fluorescence intensity at 414 nm gradually decreases, but the fluorescence intensity at 498 nm increases as OFL concentration gradually increases from 0 to 300 μM. Meanwhile, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (h-i), a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and OFL concentration is observed with the concentration ranging from 0 to 30 μM. The linear regression equation is F 2/F 1 = 0.035C [OFL] + 0.09 with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (n = 15, “C” is the concentration of analyte). Additionally, the detection limit for OFL is as low as 46 nM based on 3σ/s (“σ” is the standard deviation of blank signal, and “s” is the slope of the calibration curve) (Wang et al., 2021). In addition, the T-GQDs-based fluorescent probe for LEV has also been evaluated, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 (j). With increasing concentration of LEV, the fluorescence intensities of the two peaks have the same change trend as OFL. As shown in Fig. 4 (k-l), the fluorescence response as a function of LEV concentration shows a good linear relationship within 0–10 μM. The linear fitting equation can be fitted into F 2/F 1 = 0.042C [LEV] + 0.08 (R 2 = 0.997, n = 15). The detection limit for the LEV is calculated to be as low as 67 nM. The corresponding data are summarized in Table S3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of T-GQDs based OFL/LEV detections
ce:section.ce:para:
To clarify the ratiometric sensing mechanism, it is essential to figure out the relationship between the OFL/LEV concentration and fluorescence enhancement of T-GODs at 498 nm. Apart from the detection of OFL/LEV with T-GODs, the fluorescence spectra of OFL/LEV without T-GODs are also measured with different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5
(a) and Fig. S11 , with the increase of the OFL concentration, the fluorescence intensity of OFL increases and then decreases, which is different from the gradually rising trend during the sensing process. Moreover, the emission wavelength of OFL blue-shifts from 486 to 460 nm and remains stable with the solution concentration increasing from 0 to 300 μM, which is also distinct from the mixed solution of T-GQDs and OFL (Fig. 5 (b)). Meanwhile, the similar results are further confirmed in LEV situation ( Fig. S12 ). These results indicate that the enhancement mechanism of fluorescence intensity at 498 nm is independent of the OFL/LEV concentration and may depend on the corresponding electronic transition states in OFL/LEV affected by the presence of T-GQDs. According to previous reports, the complexation or electrostatic interaction of GQDs with other molecules usually generally leads to the electron transfer process, which further in turn influences the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (Shtepliuk, Khranovskyy, & Yakimova, 2017). Therefore, FT-IR spectra are measured to determine whether functional groups are formed or adhered between T-GQDs and OFL/LEV. As presented in Fig. 5 (c), the FT-IR spectra of T-GQDs in the presence and absence of OFL/LEV show no new absorption site appearing. However, blue shifts appear with the addition of OFL/LEV for the three absorption peaks of CO, CN and C-F groups in T-GQDs, moving from 1639 cm−1, 1339 cm−1 and 1033 cm−1 to 1618 cm−1, 1315 cm−1 and 1019 cm−1, respectively. These blue shifts are attributed to the electrostatic bonds, such as hydrogen bonds and dipole–dipole interactions, that hinder the bending vibrations of these groups (Long et al., 2017). Notably, the formation of electrostatic bonds is able to provide the basis for the intermolecular ET process (Shtepliuk, Khranovskyy, & Yakimova, 2017).

Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of T-GQDs change significantly with the increase of OFL content (Fig. 5 (d)), in which the absorption peak at 268 nm derived from π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 core disappears gradually. However, a strong absorption peak appears at 293 nm, which is confirmed to stem from OFL in the corresponding control experiment in Fig. 5 (e). It is noteworthy that the absorption intensity at 293 nm in the mixed solution of T-GQDs and OFL is significantly higher than that of the single OFL solution. This phenomenon suggests that the added OFL reduces the electron capture ability of T-GQDs, which results in a large number of electrons transfer to OFL. Compared with OFL, UV–vis absorption spectra of LEV also show similar results in Fig. S13 . Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5 (f), the electronic transition band gap of T-GQDs gradually decreases with the increase of OFL concentration. It implies that the excited electrons will radiate into a narrow bandgap for the recombination, corresponding to the fluorescence transition from 414 nm to long-wavelength 498 nm. More importantly, the UV–vis absorption band of T-GQDs is close to that of the OFL ( Fig. S14 ), which allows the electron transfer in the conduction band of T-GQDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of OFL (Li, Yu, & Jiang, 2018). Based on the above studies, we speculate that the effect of intermolecular ET through the electrostatic interaction between the T-GQDs and OFL leads to the electrons transfer from T-GQDs to OFL, resulting in the fluorescence quenching at 414 nm and the fluorescence enhancement at 498 nm (Fig. 5 (g)) (Zhao, Liu, Zhou, & Han, 2007).

The time-resolved PL spectrum reveals the deactivation kinetics of electrons from an excited state to the ground state via radiative and non-radiative pathways (Lutsyk, Piryatinski, Kachkovsky, Verbitsky, & Rozhin, 2017). Therefore, time-resolved PL spectra of T-GQDs monitored at 414 nm in the presence and absence of OFL are measured to further explore the underlying mechanism (Fig. 5 (h)). The fluorescence decay curves are well fitted by the following double-exponential equation:
(1)
I
t
=
B 1
e x p
- t / τ
1
+
B 2
e x p
- t / τ
2

where “B 1” and “B 2” are the fractional contributions to PL emission from two transition channels with the lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2, respectively. Double exponential decay lifetime values indicate that electrons have two distinct radiative transition channels (Tang, Zhang, Zhang, Xiao, Shi, Kong, & Xu, 2021). Through fitting the lifetime of T-GQDs, the channel with τ 1∼2.732 ns (channel-1) contributes 95.44 % of the PL emission weight, while the channel with τ 2∼21.174 ns (channel-2) contributes 4.56 % weight ( Fig. S15 ). Specifically, channel-1 is related to the radiative and non-radiative relaxation of electrons in T-GQDs, while channel-2 is attributed to the radiative relaxation of electrons in OFL. However, after the addition of OFL, the weight of channel-1 decreases to 87.62 %, while the weight of channel-2 increases to 12.38 %, suggesting that certain electrons are transferred from T-GQDs to OFL for the radiative recombination via intermolecular ET effect (Xu et al., 2021). Besides, the average lifetime (τ av) is calculated using the following equation:
(2)
τ
av
=
∑ i
B
i
τ
i
2
∑ i
B i
τ i

The calculated τ av of T-GQDs in the absence and presence of OFL are 3.575 ns and 5.584 ns, respectively. After the addition of OFL, the prolongation of τ av indicates an increase in the radiation path of electrons, which further verifies the electron transfer from T-GQDs to OFL.

On the other hand, monitored at 498 nm in Fig. 5 (i), time-resolved PL spectra of T-GQDs with and without OFL display mono-exponential and bi-exponential fitting, respectively. It is shown that the weight of channel-1 reduces drastically from 80.15 % to 0, while the weight of channel-2 increases greatly from 19.85 % to 100 % ( Fig. S16 ). It means that a large number of electrons are captured by OFL due to the intermolecular ET effect and produce radiative recombination at the low energy level for the generation of new fluorescence emission, resulting in the fluorescence increase at 498 nm and the fluorescence quenching at 414 nm (Zhang, Zhang, Liu, Yi, & Sun, 2015; Shang, Wang, Liu, Jiang, Liu, Chou, & Fang, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of OFL in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The applicability and practicability of the fluorescent probe in complicated real samples are further evaluated. Before the practical sample detection, it is necessary to determine whether the optical properties of T-GQDs are affected by the practical samples (juice, carbonated drink and milk). As shown in Fig. S18 , the excitation-emission matrix and the fluorescence spectra of T-GQDs in the three samples have no significant change compared with aqueous solutions, which indicates that the as-synthesized T-GQDs have practical conditions for the actual sample detection. To investigate the sensitivity of the probe under the three sample conditions, as shown in Fig. S19
(a-c), the fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs in real samples are obtained after the addition of different amounts of OFL (0–100 μM). The fluorescence responses of T-GQDs vary linearly with the OFL concentrations in juice, carbonated drink and milk samples ( Fig. S19
(d-f)) ranging from 0 to 18 μM, 0–20 μM and 0–30 μM with high correlation coefficients of 0.997, 0.977 and 0.990 (n = 15), respectively. Besides, detection limits are demonstrated to be 48 nM, 71 nM and 120 nM, respectively. Furthermore, quantitative detection of OFL in real samples is also evaluated. The average percentage recoveries in the three real samples with two concentrations of OFL (5 and 10 μM) are in the acceptable range of 93.5 %-103.8 %, and all relative standard deviation (RSDs) are found lower than 5.9 %. In addition, the LEV is also evaluated to be reliable in the same method with all the data summarized in Table S4 . The above results demonstrate that the T-GQDs based fluorescent ratiometric sensors can be used for the effectively quantitative detection of OFL/LEV in real sample with good accuracy, reliability and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual assay of OFL and LEV using ratiometric fluorescent test paper
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to facilitate the visualization of OFL and LEV detections, a novel intelligent real-time sensing system based on the fluorescent test paper and RGB color analysis software is designed to reduce the need for bulky detection equipment and improve the portability. The process of collecting green (G) and blue (B) values of the test paper with different concentrations of OFL using RGB analysis software are illustrated in Fig. 6
(a). As shown in Fig. 6 (b) and Video S1 , the test papers titrated by OFL and LEV emit bright cyan fluorescence, while the others only show dim dark blue fluorescence, suggesting the high selectivity of T-GQDs-based fluorescent test paper. Moreover, with the increasing concentration of OFL (0–50 μM) and LEV (0–100 μM), the fluorescence color of the test paper gradually changes from blue-purple to cyan (Fig. 6 (c)), which is consistent with the mixed solution of T-GQDs and OFL under the UV light irradiation ( Fig. S20 ). Furthermore, as displayed in Fig. 6 (d), the ratiometric fluorescent test paper is successfully applied to detect OFL in the real samples, which is rare in other literature about FQs detections. The corresponding sunlight photos are shown in Fig. S21 . Based on the above phenomena, a smartphone application (ColorDesk) is used as a chroma signal collector and analyzer to realize the quantitative determination of OFL. Fig. 6 (e) displays a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.993) between the G/B ratio and the concentration of OFL, and the detection limit is calculated to be 74 nM. Besides, the practical application capability of the system is further assessed in Fig. 6 (f) and Video S2 . The obtained concentration of OFL is 50.9 μM, which is very close to the standard value of 50 μM. Therefore, the above results confirm that our designed low-cost sensing system has high feasibility for the detection of OFL/LEV in daily life applications.

In summary, we synthesize T-GQDs with a blue emission by the one-pot hydrothermal method using norfloxacin and phosphoric as precursors. When OFL/LEV are added into the T-GQDs solution, double peak emissions (414 nm and 498 nm), rather than a single peak, emerge in PL spectra excited by a 334 nm wavelength laser, and the fluorescence intensities of these two peaks compete with each other as the concentration of OFL/LEV increases. Fluorescence measurements for the ratiometric detection of OFL/LEV within 0–30 μM/0–10 μM are performed based on the T-GQDs, and the detection limit is calculated to be 46 nM for OFL and 67 nM for LEV. Data analysis obviously reveals that the intermolecular ET effect is the key to the construction of the ratiometric detection method. In the presence of OFL/LEV, the excited electrons of T-GQDs can be transferred to OFL/LEV by the electrostatic bonds, resulting in the fluorescence quenching at 414 nm and the fluorescence enhancement at 498 nm. This ratiometric sensing based on the special intermolecular ET effect has better selectivity and more powerful sensing ability. Besides, the method has been validated for monitoring OFL/LEV in real samples and applied to paper-based visual sensor monitoring. Most interestingly, the RGB color analysis of test paper can be identified by the smartphone application, which provides a simple, instrument-free and on-site visual analysis. These results reported here prospect a bright future of fluorescent colorimetric test papers and smart sensing for extensive applications in the assays of environment, medicine and food.

Changxing Wang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Feifei Qin: Formal analysis. Siyuan Tang: Data curation. Xiameng Li: Investigation. Tingting Li: Methodology. Guoqiang Guo: Software. Chenjie Gu: Supervision, Validation. Xu Wang: Writing – review & editing. Da Chen: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135514.
